items.q: Is the equator hotter because it is closer to the Sun?
items.correct: No — the equator is hotter because the Sun's rays strike more directly, concentrating energy on a smaller area and passing through less atmosphere.
items.misconception: Tropical regions are hot because they are significantly nearer to the Sun.
items.random_wrong: The equator is hot because of heat escaping from volcanoes.
items.why: The distance explanation is a classic misconception; angle of incidence is the real cause of the latitudinal energy surplus.

items.q: Does high pressure mean stormy weather?
items.correct: No — in anticyclones air descends and warms, suppressing cloud formation, so conditions are usually dry and settled; storms come with low pressure systems.
items.misconception: High pressure presses harder on clouds and squeezes more rain out of them.
items.random_wrong: High pressure only occurs at night.
items.why: Students reverse the pressure-weather link; rising air in depressions, not sinking air in anticyclones, produces rain.

items.q: Is a front just a line where rain happens?
items.correct: A front is a boundary between air masses of different temperature and humidity; rain occurs because the warmer, lighter air is forced to rise and cool along it.
items.misconception: Fronts are bands of rain that move around independently of air masses.
items.random_wrong: Fronts are barriers built to keep out cold weather.
items.why: Students memorise the map symbol without grasping the air-mass mechanism that causes uplift and precipitation.

items.q: Which circulation cell lies between the equator and roughly 30 degrees latitude?
items.why: It's The Hadley cell. “The Ferrel cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A tropical maritime air mass reaching the British Isles is typically:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Warm and moist. “Cold and dry” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Warm air rises gradually over colder air, giving layered cloud and steady rain
items.misconception: Cold air rises steeply over warm air
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Warm air rises gradually over colder air, giving layered cloud and steady rain. “Cold air rises steeply over warm air” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Winter anticyclones over the British Isles often bring:
items.correct: Cold, clear conditions with frost and fog
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cold, clear conditions with frost and fog. “Prolonged heavy rainfall” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Temperature falls with height at an average environmental lapse rate of about 6.5°C per 1,000 m. If it is 15°C at sea level, what is the temperature at 2,000 m in °C?
items.why: The real figure is 2 °C. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
